Capstone project: Providing data-driven suggestions for HR¶

Description and deliverables¶

This capstone project is an opportunity for you to analyze a dataset and build predictive models that can provide insights to the Human Resources (HR) department of a large consulting firm.

Upon completion, you will have two artifacts that you will be able to present to future employers. One is a brief one-page summary of this project that you will present to external stakeholders as the data professional in Salifort Motors. The other is a complete code notebook provided here. Based on your prior coursework, select one method to complete this project: use either a regression model or a machine learning model to predict whether an employee will leave the company. The exemplar following this actiivty shows both approaches, but you only need to do one.

In your deliverables, you will include the model evaluation (and interpretation if applicable), a data visualization(s) of your choice that is directly related to the question you ask, ethical considerations, and the resources you used to troubleshoot and find answers or solutions.

PACE stages¶

Pace: Plan¶

Consider the questions in your PACE Strategy Document to reflect on the Plan stage.

In this stage, consider the following:

Understand the business scenario and problem¶

The HR department at Salifort Motors wants to take some initiatives to improve employee satisfaction levels at the company. They collected data from employees, but now they don’t know what to do with it. They refer to you as a data analytics professional and ask you to provide data-driven suggestions based on your understanding of the data. They have the following question: what’s likely to make the employee leave the company?

Your goals in this project are to analyze the data collected by the HR department and to build a model that predicts whether or not an employee will leave the company.

If you can predict employees likely to quit, it might be possible to identify factors that contribute to their leaving. Because it is time-consuming and expensive to find, interview, and hire new employees, increasing employee retention will be beneficial to the company.

Familiarize yourself with the HR dataset¶

The dataset that you'll be using in this lab contains 15,000 rows and 10 columns for the variables listed below.

Note: you don't need to download any data to complete this lab. For more information about the data, refer to its source on Kaggle.

Variable Description
satisfaction_level Employee-reported job satisfaction level [0–1]
last_evaluation Score of employee's last performance review [0–1]
number_project Number of projects employee contributes to
average_monthly_hours Average number of hours employee worked per month
time_spend_company How long the employee has been with the company (years)
Work_accident Whether or not the employee experienced an accident while at work
left Whether or not the employee left the company
promotion_last_5years Whether or not the employee was promoted in the last 5 years
Department The employee's department
salary The employee's salary (U.S. dollars)

💭

Reflect on these questions as you complete the plan stage.¶

  • Who are your stakeholders for this project?
  • What are you trying to solve or accomplish?
  • What are your initial observations when you explore the data?
  • What resources do you find yourself using as you complete this stage?
  • Do you have any ethical considerations in this stage?

1) My stakeholders are the HR team and other relevant employees and stakeholders for the Salifort Motors company. 2) I'm trying to make a successful model at predicting whether an employee will leave the company or not. 3) My first observation is that it includes many factors but the data does not completely encompass possible reasons why an employee has left the company. In the future, it may be useful to pursue data from exit surveys to investigate why employees have left the company. 4) I have not used any resources yet, but plan to use my previous python labs from my Waze portfolio project, in which I followed a similar plan to the project proposal I set up for this project. 5) I don't have any ethical concerns at this time because the data is anonymized, so all identifying personal information has been removed. Additionally, this project is aimed at improving employees' experiences and satisfaction levels, not for hiring purposes or for making decisions about employment status.

Step 1. Imports¶

  • Import packages
  • Load dataset

Import packages¶

In [1]:
# Import general packages

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Import visualization packages

import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Packages for Logistic Regression, Confusion Matrix, and metrics

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score, roc_curve, auc
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score,\
f1_score, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay, RocCurveDisplay, PrecisionRecallDisplay

# Import data modeling packages

from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from xgboost import XGBClassifier
from xgboost import plot_importance

# Import package to save models

import pickle

Load dataset¶

Pandas is used to read a dataset called HR_capstone_dataset.csv. As shown in this cell, the dataset has been automatically loaded in for you. You do not need to download the .csv file, or provide more code, in order to access the dataset and proceed with this lab. Please continue with this activity by completing the following instructions.

In [2]:
# RUN THIS CELL TO IMPORT YOUR DATA. 

# Load dataset into a dataframe
df0 = pd.read_csv("HR_capstone_dataset.csv")


# Display first few rows of the dataframe

df0.head()

# Make a copy of the original dataset

df = df0.copy()

Step 2. Data Exploration (Initial EDA and data cleaning)¶

  • Understand your variables
  • Clean your dataset (missing data, redundant data, outliers)

Rename columns¶

As a data cleaning step, rename the columns as needed. Standardize the column names so that they are all in snake_case, correct any column names that are misspelled, and make column names more concise as needed.

# Import libraries to clean column names 

from skimpy import clean_columns
In [9]:
# Rename columns as needed

# Change column names to snake_case for uniformity 
df = clean_columns(df)

# Rename select columns for consistency and to fix spelling mistakes

df = df.rename(columns={'satisfaction_level' : 'satisfaction_score', 
                        'last_evaluation' : 'performance_score',
                        'number_project' : 'projects',
                        'average_montly_hours' : 'hours_per_month',
                        'time_spend_company' : 'tenure',
                        'promotion_last_5years' : 'recent_promotion'})

# Display all column names after the update
list(df.columns)

In [14]:
# Explore engineered features 

# Create column for total hours worked over the entire tenure (by multiplying hours/month and tenure in years times months in a year)
df['total_hours'] = df['hours_per_month'] * df['tenure'] * 12

# Describe new column
df['total_hours'].describe()
Out[14]:
count    11991.000000
mean      8174.790093
std       4083.536104
min       2304.000000
25%       5292.000000
50%       6912.000000
75%       9708.000000
max      34080.000000
Name: total_hours, dtype: float64
In [15]:
# Create a binary column labeled full time where employees receive a 1 (yes) if they work greater than 130 hours/month
# 30-40 hrs/week is the average workweek, so 120-160 hrs/month is the average hours of work in a month. 
# 130 hours/month is a common threshold for part time vs full time. 

df['full_time'] = np.where(df['hours_per_month'] > 130, 1, 0)

# Describe new column
df['full_time'].describe()
Out[15]:
count    11991.000000
mean         0.948878
std          0.220255
min          0.000000
25%          1.000000
50%          1.000000
75%          1.000000
max          1.000000
Name: full_time, dtype: float64
In [16]:
# Create a binary column labeled hard worker where employees receive a 1 (yes) if they work greater than 240 hours/month
# 240 hours is 1.5 times the threshold for a full-time employee (160 hrs/month)

df['hard_worker'] = np.where(df['hours_per_month'] > 240, 1, 0)

# Describe new column
df['hard_worker'].describe()
Out[16]:
count    11991.000000
mean         0.267117
std          0.442472
min          0.000000
25%          0.000000
50%          0.000000
75%          1.000000
max          1.000000
Name: hard_worker, dtype: float64
In [17]:
# Create a binary column labeled high performer where employees receive a 1 (yes) if they earned a performance score of greater than or equal to 0.9 or have had a recent promotion (1)

df['high_performer'] = np.where((df['performance_score'] >= 0.9) | (df['recent_promotion'] == 1), 1, 0)

# Describe new column
df['high_performer'].describe()
Out[17]:
count    11991.000000
mean         0.207739
std          0.405706
min          0.000000
25%          0.000000
50%          0.000000
75%          0.000000
max          1.000000
Name: high_performer, dtype: float64
In [18]:
# Create a new column labeled happy worker where employees receive a 1 (yes) if their satisfaction_score is greater or equal to 0.7

df['happy_worker'] = np.where(df['satisfaction_score'] >= 0.7, 1, 0)

# Describe new column
df['happy_worker'].describe()
Out[18]:

Check outliers¶

Check for outliers in the data.

In [19]:
# Create a boxplot to visualize distribution of `time_spend_company` and detect any outliers

plt.boxplot(df['tenure'], vert=False, medianprops=dict(color='red', linewidth=2))
plt.xlim(0, 12)
plt.title('Distribution of tenure')
plt.xlabel('Years')
plt.show();
In [20]:
# Determine the number of rows containing outliers

# Calculate the quantile values and the interquantile range

Q1 = df['tenure'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df['tenure'].quantile(0.75)

IQR = Q3 - Q1

print(IQR)

# Calculate the upper and lower limits for outliers 

lower_limit = Q1 - (1.5 * IQR)
upper_limit = Q3 + (1.5 * IQR)

print(lower_limit, upper_limit)

# Find rows containing outliers and create a mask

outliers_tenure = (df['tenure'] < lower_limit) | (df['tenure'] > upper_limit)

# Calculate the number of rows containing outliers

outliers_tenure.sum()

1.0
1.5 5.5
Out[20]:
824

Certain types of models are more sensitive to outliers than others. When you get to the stage of building your model, consider whether to remove outliers, based on the type of model you decide to use.

In [21]:
# Create a copy of the dataset without outliers to use for models with this requirement
# Reset the index so rows are relabeled in continuous counting order

df_no_outliers = df[~outliers_tenure].reset_index(drop = True)

df_no_outliers.info()

# Determine the number of rows containing outliers for total_hours

# Calculate the quantile values and the interquantile range

Q1 = df_no_outliers['total_hours'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df_no_outliers['total_hours'].quantile(0.75)

IQR = Q3 - Q1

print(IQR)

# Calculate the upper and lower limits for outliers 

lower_limit = Q1 - (1.5 * IQR)
upper_limit = Q3 + (1.5 * IQR)

print(lower_limit, upper_limit)

# Find rows containing outliers and create a mask

outliers_total_hours = (df_no_outliers['total_hours'] < lower_limit) | (df_no_outliers['total_hours'] > upper_limit)

# Calculate the number of rows containing outliers

print(outliers_total_hours.sum())

# Calculate the percentage of all rows in the dataset that have outliers

outliers_total_hours.sum() / df_no_outliers['total_hours'].count()

pAce: Analyze Stage¶

  • Perform EDA (analyze relationships between variables)

💭

Reflect on these questions as you complete the analyze stage.¶

  • What did you observe about the relationships between variables?
  • What do you observe about the distributions in the data?
  • What transformations did you make with your data? Why did you chose to make those decisions?
  • What are some purposes of EDA before constructing a predictive model?
  • What resources do you find yourself using as you complete this stage?
  • Do you have any ethical considerations in this stage?
  1. Surprisingly there is no relationship between satisfaction_score and performance_score.
  2. Satisfaction scores vary widely among employees. Most of the values are above 0.5, but there is a cluster of employees with very low satisfaction scores. No employees received very low performance scores (lower than 0.3); the scores are fairly evenly distributed above 0.5, and a small amount of employees scores lower than this. Very few employees have had recent promotions, and less than 10% of employees earn a high salary; this may impact employee retention.
  3. I engineered some new features that I thought might have predictive value for the models.
  4. An EDA allows me to become familiar with the data that I have available to use in models. I am able to check for potential issues and errors with the data. I can check for several presumptions before creating a logistic regression model.
  5. I intend to use my previous data project to assist with planning of the EDA and proper syntax.
  6. I do not have any ethical considerations in this stage. Information is not linked to specific individuals and the purpose of the project is to improve employee experiences. I do not expect it to cause harm to any individuals.

Step 2. Data Exploration (Continue EDA)¶

Begin by understanding how many employees left and what percentage of all employees this figure represents.

In [29]:
# Get numbers of people who left vs. stayed

print(df['left'].value_counts())

# Get percentages of people who left vs. stayed

print(df['left'].value_counts(normalize=True))

0    10000
1     1991
Name: left, dtype: int64
0    0.833959
1    0.166041
Name: left, dtype: float64

print(df_no_outliers['left'].value_counts())

# Get percentages of people who left vs. stayed

print(df_no_outliers['left'].value_counts(normalize=True))

1    1671

Data visualizations¶

Now, examine variables that you're interested in, and create plots to visualize relationships between variables in the data.

In [31]:
# List columns

df.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
Int64Index: 11991 entries, 0 to 11999
Data columns (total 15 columns):
 #   Column              Non-Null Count  Dtype

plt.title('Histogram of satisfaction_score')

plt.ylabel('count')
plt.legend() 
plt.show()

plt.pie(data,
        labels=[f'{data.index[0]}: {data.values[0]}',
                f'{data.index[1]}: {data.values[1]}'],
        autopct='%1.1f%%'
        )
plt.title('Count of work_accident');

In [52]:
# Most of the departments ratios of retained to left employees are within a close range (14-16%)
# RandD and management have higher retention levels than other departments
# HR has a slightly higher left levels than other departments

percent_df_no_outliers = pd.crosstab(df_no_outliers['department'], df_no_outliers['left'], normalize='index').mul(100).round(2)

# Rename columns for clarity if needed
percent_df_no_outliers.columns = ['retained', 'left'] 

percent_df_no_outliers

In [53]:
# Does department significantly affect whether an employee stays or leaves? 

# Null Hypothesis: The rate of employee retention vs leaving does not signifcantly vary by department type. 
# Alternative Hypothesis: The rate of employee retention vs leaving does significantly vary by department type. 

# Import library for a chi-square test
from scipy.stats import chi2_contingency

# Perform a chi-square test for df_no_outliers
chi2_statistic, p_value, degrees_of_freedom, expected_frequencies = chi2_contingency(percent_df_no_outliers[['retained', 'left']])

print(f"Chi2 Statistic: {chi2_statistic}")
print(f"P-value: {p_value}")
print(f"Degrees of Freedom: {degrees_of_freedom}")
print("Expected Frequencies Table:")
print(expected_frequencies)

Chi2 Statistic: 3.2558430620289003
P-value: 0.9532821040215409
Degrees of Freedom: 9
Expected Frequencies Table:
[[84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]
 [84.981 15.019]]
In [54]:
# Recreate the process for the general dataframe (with outliers)
percent_df = pd.crosstab(df['department'], df['left'], normalize='index').mul(100).round(2)

# Rename columns for clarity if needed
percent_df.columns = ['retained', 'left'] 

# Perform a chi-square test for no_outliers data
chi2_statistic, p_value, degrees_of_freedom, expected_frequencies = chi2_contingency(percent_df[['retained', 'left']])

print(f"Chi2 Statistic: {chi2_statistic}")
print(f"P-value: {p_value}")
print(f"Degrees of Freedom: {degrees_of_freedom}")
print("Expected Frequencies Table:")
print(expected_frequencies)

Chi2 Statistic: 3.361596902042104
P-value: 0.9482198280334139
Degrees of Freedom: 9
Expected Frequencies Table:
[[83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]
 [83.912 16.088]]
In [55]:
"""
Interpretation of Chi-Square test: 

The test shows that I cannot accept the alternative hypothesis, meaning I fail to reject the null hypothesis, 
because the p-value is 0.953 for df_no_outliers and 0.948, which both are much greater than my threshold of significance at 0.05. 
The scores of employee retention versus leaving do not significantly vary by department. This indicates that 
it is very unlikely that department type has any affect on employee retention. No employee in one department is
statistically more likely to leave than any other department, even though the actual reported rates of retention 
do vary. 

Impact: 

As a result, I have chosen to not move forward with transforming this variable to many numerical variables in 
order to complete a logistic regression. To complete this model, it is presumed that all variables will be 
correlated with the target variable, "left", Thus, I will drop this variable from df_no_outliers, 
as it is not usable for a logistic regression. While the machine learning models do not
this assumption, transforming this variable into a numerical data type would lead to many new variables created
and could harm the model leading to overfitting, so I have also removed it from the original df. 

However, this finding is informational for the hr department regardless of the model because they are able to infer 
that there is not a significant problem only in one or two departments to focus on. It suggests a solution to raise 
employee retention rates may be applied more broadly across the company and not tailored individually to departments. 

"""
Out[55]:
'\nInterpretation of Chi-Square test: \n\nThe test shows that I cannot accept the alternative hypothesis, meaning I fail to reject the null hypothesis, \nbecause the p-value is 0.953 for df_no_outliers and 0.948, which both are much greater than my threshold of significance at 0.05. \nThe scores of employee retention versus leaving do not significantly vary by department. This indicates that \nit is very unlikely that department type has any affect on employee retention. No employee in one department is\nstatistically more likely to leave than any other department, even though the actual reported rates of retention \ndo vary. \n\nImpact: \n\nAs a result, I have chosen to not move forward with transforming this variable to many numerical variables in \norder to complete a logistic regression. To complete this model, it is presumed that all variables will be \ncorrelated with the target variable, "left", Thus, I will drop this variable from df_no_outliers, \nas it is not usable for a logistic regression. While the machine learning models do not\nthis assumption, transforming this variable into a numerical data type would lead to many new variables created\nand could harm the model leading to overfitting, so I have also removed it from the original df. \n\nHowever, this finding is informational for the hr department regardless of the model because they are able to infer \nthat there is not a significant problem only in one or two departments to focus on. It suggests a solution to raise \nemployee retention rates may be applied more broadly across the company and not tailored individually to departments. \n\n'

In [59]:
# I have noticed that a far smaller percentage of high salary employees left the company than low salary employees.
# This indicates that there possibly could be a correlation between salary and left.
In [60]:
# Transform the `salary` column from string into an ordinal numeric category 
# low = 0, medium=1, high=2 
df['salary'] = (df['salary'].astype('category').cat.set_categories(['low', 'medium', 'high']).cat.codes)

# Check the first few df rows

df.head()

In [64]:
# Show the relationship between recent_promotion, tenure, and left

sns.relplot(data=df_no_outliers, x="performance_score", y="satisfaction_score", hue="left", alpha=0.3)
plt.title('Retention of employees based on satisfaction and performance scores')
plt.show();
In [65]:
# Insights: 
# While similar to the previous graph, this scatterplot reveals several interesting relationships
#1) Those with performance scores from 0.4-0.6 and satisfaction scores of around 0.35-0.5. 
#2) Employees that perform well (0.75+), but are very unsatisfied with their work (under 0.15)
#3) A large population of employees that have performance scores and satisfaction scores over 0.5
#   are less likely to leave the company. 
#4) A cluster of employees who leave have both high satisfaction and high performance scores
In [66]:

plt.title('Employee Promotions based on hours_per_month and performance scores')
plt.show();

# Show the relationship between total_hours, satisfaction score, and left

sns.relplot(data=df_no_outliers, x="total_hours", y="satisfaction_score", hue="left", alpha=0.3)
plt.title('Retention of employees based on total_hours and satisfaction scores')
plt.show();
In [70]:
# Insights: 
# 1) Overall, employees are more satisfied when they have worked less than 10,000 hours for the company,
#    and they are less likely to leave the company. 
# 2) A cluster of moderately satisfied employees that work 5,000-6,000 hours over their time with the company are
#    more likely to leave the company. 
# 3) Veru disatisfied employees who have worked over 11,500 hours are likely to leave the company.
# 4) A cluster of employees who are satisfied after working many hours for the company still leave the company.
In [71]:
# Create a scatterplot to show the relationship between satisfaction score and left
plt.scatter(df_no_outliers['satisfaction_score'], df_no_outliers['left'])
plt.xlabel('satisfaction_score')
plt.ylabel('left')
plt.title('Relationship between satisfaction_score and left')
plt.show();
In [72]:
# Create a scatterplot to show the relationship between hours_per_month and left
plt.scatter(df_no_outliers['hours_per_month'], df_no_outliers['left'])
plt.xlabel('hours_per_month')
plt.ylabel('left')
plt.title('Relationship between hours_per_month and left')
plt.show();
In [73]:
# Insights:
# 1) Satisfied employees generally stay working with the company regardless of how many hours they work per month. 
# 2) Moderately satisfied employees that work 125-175 hrs a month are more likely to leave the company. 
# 3) Practically all employees who work over 290 hours a month end up leaving the company, regardless of their 
#    satisfaction levels. 
# 4) Employees who are very unsatisfied and work around 250 to 300 hours a month are likely to leave the company. 
# 5) Most employees that work under 130 hours per month are retained by the company. This is a common threshold for 
#    part time vs full time workers, though some employers set a higher threshold . Part time employees are much more likely than full-time workers to stay with the company. 
# 6) However, the second graph does not show a direct linear correlation between satisfaction score
#    and left. Employees are retained and leave the company across all satisfaction scores.

In [79]:
# Show the relationship between `hours_per_month` and `recent_promotion`
plt.figure(figsize=(16, 3))
sns.scatterplot(data=df_no_outliers, x='hours_per_month', y='recent_promotion', hue='left', alpha=0.4)
plt.title('Employee Retention based on Hours per month and recent promotion (within last 5 yrs)')
Out[79]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Employee Retention based on Hours per month and recent promotion (within last 5 yrs)')
In [80]:
# Insights:
# 1) Across all three salary types, employees are more likely to leave the company if they work more hours per month. 
# 2) Employees from all salary groups that are less satisfied are more likely to leave. 
# 3) Performance score does not seem to affect the rate of retention across all salary groups. 
# 4) Having 7 projects affects the rate of leaving the company across all salary groups. Based on an additional scatterplot,
#    all employees with 7 projects, regardless of how many hours per month they worked, left the company. Employees left the 
#    company who had all numbers of projects, but having 6 projects also led to a higher rate of leaving. Additionally, employees
#    working higher hours per month across all project numbers were more likely to leave the company. 
# 5) Very few employees working the highest hours_per_month received promotions. These employees were more likely to leave
#    than employees working lower amounts of hours.
# 6) Employees who have been promoted within the last 5 years are less likely to leave the company.
In [81]:

df_no_outliers.columns

       'high_performer', 'happy_worker'],
      dtype='object')
In [82]:
# Define variables for the pairplot

x_vars = ['satisfaction_score', 'performance_score', 'projects',
       'hours_per_month', 'tenure', 'work_accident', 'left',
       'recent_promotion', 'salary', 'total_hours', 'full_time', 'hard_worker',
       'high_performer', 'happy_worker']
y_vars = ['left']
In [83]:
# Complete a selective pairplot for all columns agains left. 

%matplotlib inline
sns.pairplot(data = df_no_outliers, x_vars = x_vars, y_vars = y_vars)
plt.show();

happy_worker 0.800677 0.148159 0.036942 0.086843 -0.065793 0.020010 -0.243401 0.023078 0.013687 0.000744 0.110875 0.000301 0.062936 1.000000
In [85]:
# Create a correlation heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(15,10))
sns.heatmap(df_no_outliers.corr(method='pearson'), vmin=-1, vmax=1, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title('Correlation heatmap of df_no_outliers',
          fontsize=18)
plt.show();

Insights¶

I was left with the following impressions: 1) Employees who leave tend to be unsatisfied and overworked (work very high average hours_per_month and/or have many projects). Employees who are satsified do leave the company, but these employees do tend to be overworked. The other significant category of people who leave are those with moderately low performance scores and moderately low satisfaction scores. 2) Employees who are satisfied are less likely to leave the company. 3) Employees within the high salary category are less likely than the low and medium salary employees to leave the company. However, employees form all salary categories are likely to leave the company if they are overworked or have 6-7 projects. Disatisfied employees are more likely to leave the company, regardless of what category salary they are in. 4) Employees working under 130 hours per month (part-time) are less likely to leave the company than employees who work longer monthly hours. 5) Employees who have received recent promotions (within the last 5 years) are less likely to leave the company. The employees who work the longest hours per month for the company are the least likely to receive promotions and these employees also frequently leave the company. 6) Employees across all departments are equally likely to leave the company. The difference in retention rates among departments is not statistically significant. Department is not a predictive variable to base a model on. The HR department can conclude that this evidence suggests that there is not a major issue among specific departments affecting employee retention rate. This also suggests the HR department can focus implementing solutions more broadly among the employee population than tailoring the approach to select departments.
In [86]:
# Visualize the distribution of left among number of projects

pd.crosstab(df_no_outliers['projects'],df_no_outliers['left']).plot.bar(stacked=True)
plt.title('Left by Number of Projects')
plt.ylabel('count')
plt.show();
In [87]:
# Add a binary variable to identify if an employee is overworked
# Overwork will be defined as working for 1.5X the normal hours a month for full time(1.5*130=195), working 6-7 projects
df['overworked'] = np.where((df['hours_per_month'] >= 195) | (df['projects'] >= 6), 1, 0)

df['overworked'].describe()

df_no_outliers['overworked'] = np.where((df_no_outliers['hours_per_month'] >= 195) | (df_no_outliers['projects'] >= 6), 1, 0)

df_no_outliers['overworked'].describe()

In [89]:
# Add a binary variable to identify if an employee is underappreciated
# underappreciated will be defined as having only 2 projects, having a high performance score and no promotion in the last 5 years

df['under_appreciated'] = np.where((df['projects'] == 2) | ((df['performance_score'] > 0.9) & (df['recent_promotion'] == 0)), 1, 0)

df['under_appreciated'].describe()

# Repeat process for the no outlier df

df_no_outliers['under_appreciated'] = np.where((df_no_outliers['projects'] == 2) | ((df_no_outliers['performance_score'] > 0.9) & (df_no_outliers['recent_promotion'] == 0)), 1, 0)

df_no_outliers['under_appreciated'].describe()

In [91]:
# I have concerns about 2 aspects regarding the variables. 

# 1) Satisfaction_score- These are self-reported and the only subjective variable in the dataset. I am concerned about
#    creating a model that is heavily influenced by this variable. The company may not have accurate information about 
#    satisfaction scores for all employees, as with voluntary surveys typically the individuals with the strongest feelings
#    are more likely to respond than neutral individuals, and the information the company gets may not necessarily be true,
#    as employees may be reluctant to report their true feelings. The variable I created called happy_worker also relies on 
#    this variable. The correlation heatmap shows that satisfaction socres is predicted to be very influential in the model.
#    We already know that higher employee satisfaction has an overall positive relationship to employees being retained by 
#    the company. In the future, the company may not have this variable for all employees. I will build a model without this
#    variable.

# 2) Hours_per_month- I am mildly concerned that this could be impacted because it is possible that employees who are planning 
#    to quit but haven't given notice could deliberately work less hours. If employees are contracted a certain number of hours
#    per week, the company could use the contracted number of hours. However, its more complicated with salaried employees. 
#    Ensuring the company uses the average number of hours worked monthly since the beginning of their employment would 
#    help reduce the impact of this on the model's quality. This variable should have a much lower impact on the model created, 
#    so I will retain it within the dataset.
In [92]:
# Drop the satisfaction_score variable and happy worker from both dfs

df = df.drop(columns=['satisfaction_score', 'happy_worker'])

df_no_outliers = df_no_outliers.drop(columns=['satisfaction_score', 'happy_worker'])

# Create a 2nd correlation heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(15,10))
sns.heatmap(df_no_outliers.corr(method='pearson'), vmin=-1, vmax=1, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.title('Correlation heatmap of df_no_outliers',
          fontsize=18)
plt.show();

paCe: Construct Stage¶

  • Determine which models are most appropriate
  • Construct the model
  • Confirm model assumptions
  • Evaluate model results to determine how well your model fits the data

🔎

Recall model assumptions¶

Logistic Regression model assumptions

  • Outcome variable is categorical
  • Observations are independent of each other
  • No severe multicollinearity among X variables
  • No extreme outliers
  • Linear relationship between each X variable and the logit of the outcome variable
  • Sufficiently large sample size

💭

Reflect on these questions as you complete the constructing stage.¶

  • Which independent variables did you choose for the model and why?
  • Are each of the assumptions met?
  • How well does your model fit the data?
  • Can you improve it? Is there anything you would change about the model?
  • What resources do you find yourself using as you complete this stage?
  • Do you have any ethical considerations in this stage?

1) I am going to run multiple models and compare. This data is appropriate for a binary logistic regression model, so I can run a logistic regression model and tree-based machine learning models, including decision tree and random forest. For the logistic regression, I need to be cautious about multicollinearity among the X variables. When variables have a Pearson correlation coefficient value greater than the absolute value of 0.7 with each other, these variables are strongly multicollinear and I will select the variable that has the highest correlation with 'left', which is the binary target variable. I am using the df_no_outliers for the logistic regression model.

The following variables are strongly multicollinear:

  • hours per month & hard worker (0.75)
  • hours per month & overworked (0.83)
  • tenure & total hours (0.76)

Out of these variables, I have selected hard worker, overworked and tenure to be used in the X variables. Hours per month and total hours must be dropped from df_no_outliers. The X variables will be hard worker, overworked, tenure, performance scores, projects, work accident, recent promotion, salary, full time, high performer, overworked, and under appreciated. The y variable will be left.

2) All the assumptions I can make prior to actually building the model have been met.

3) The data fits all the models well. They all do an acceptable job at correctly identifying the individuals who will leave (recall score). My champion model is the random forest model because it has the best combination of scores for meeting the company's needs.

4) The model could potentially be improved but it would take more computational time and resources. This could be done by obtaining additional data, creating more engineered variables, and varying the parameters.

5) At this stage, I have used previous projects to guide the process of creating a model and check the correct syntax to use in python.

6) I do not have any ethical concerns at this stage. The model will likely improve the experience for employees at the company. Any incorrect guesses the model makes likely won't cause any harm and will result in the same outcome as not having the model- employees leaving the company, or individuals who would have stayed at the company receiving interventions that generally increase the rate of employees staying at the company.

Step 3. Model Building, Step 4. Results and Evaluation¶

  • Fit a model that predicts the outcome variable using two or more independent variables
  • Check model assumptions
  • Evaluate the model

Identify the type of prediction task.¶

This is a binary classification task.

Identify the types of models most appropriate for this task.¶

The models most appropriate for this task are a logistic regression model and tree-based machine learning models, including a decision tree model and a random forest model.

Modeling¶

Add as many cells as you need to conduct the modeling process.

In [95]:
# 1) Logistic Regression Model
In [96]:
# Isolate the X (predictor) variables by dropping hours per month, total hours, left (target)

X = df_no_outliers.drop(columns = ['hours_per_month', 'total_hours', 'left'])

# Isolate the target variable

y = df_no_outliers['left']
In [97]:
# Split the data into train and test sets

X_tr, X_test, y_tr, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, stratify=y,
                                              test_size=0.2, random_state=42)
In [98]:
# Split into train and validate sets
# The final ratio will be 60% train, 20% validation, 20% test. 

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(X_tr, y_tr, stratify=y_tr,
                                                  test_size=0.25, random_state=42)
In [99]:
# Check the number of samples in each 

for x in [X_train, X_val, X_test]:
    print(len(x))

6519
2173
2173
In [100]:
# Instantiate the logistic regression model using scikit-learn

model = LogisticRegression(penalty='none', max_iter=400)

model.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[100]:
LogisticRegression(C=1.0, class_weight=None, dual=False, fit_intercept=True,
                   intercept_scaling=1, l1_ratio=None, max_iter=400,
                   multi_class='auto', n_jobs=None, penalty='none',
                   random_state=None, solver='lbfgs', tol=0.0001, verbose=0,
                   warm_start=False)
In [101]:
# Get the coefficients of each variable using the .coef_ attribute on the model

pd.Series(model.coef_[0], index=X.columns)
Out[101]:
performance_score   -1.213817
projects             1.244696
tenure               1.226441
work_accident       -1.223923
recent_promotion     4.619835
salary              -0.499451
full_time            0.601105
hard_worker          1.784159
high_performer      -5.184153
overworked          -1.077894
under_appreciated    6.851299
dtype: float64
In [102]:
# Get the intercept of the model

model.intercept_
Out[102]:
array([-12.11116646])
In [103]:
# Check the final assumption for the logistic regression model by making a regplot
# there must be a linear relationship between X and the estimated log odds(logits)

# Get the predicted probabilities of the training data
training_probabilities = model.predict_proba(X_train)
training_probabilities
Out[103]:
array([[3.01039440e-01, 6.98960560e-01],
       [8.15654418e-01, 1.84345582e-01],
       [9.48357937e-01, 5.16420626e-02],
       ...,
       [9.99686085e-01, 3.13915433e-04],
       [9.87488231e-01, 1.25117694e-02],
       [8.92734685e-01, 1.07265315e-01]])
In [104]:
# Create a logit_data df containing a copy of the X_train df

logit_data = X_train.copy()

# Add a logit column to the new df

logit_data['logit'] = [np.log(prob[1] / prob[0]) for prob in training_probabilities]
In [105]:
# Create regplots for the X variables

sns.regplot(x='hard_worker', y='logit', data=logit_data, scatter_kws={'s': 2, 'alpha': 0.5})
plt.title('Log-odds: hard_worker');
In [106]:
sns.regplot(x='overworked', y='logit', data=logit_data, scatter_kws={'s': 2, 'alpha': 0.5})
plt.title('Log-odds: overworked');
In [107]:
sns.regplot(x='tenure', y='logit', data=logit_data, scatter_kws={'s': 2, 'alpha': 0.5})
plt.title('Log-odds: tenure');

sns.regplot(x='performance_score', y='logit', data=logit_data, scatter_kws={'s': 2, 'alpha': 0.5})
plt.title('Log-odds: performance_score');

plt.title('Log-odds: projects');

plt.title('Log-odds: work_accident');

sns.regplot(x='recent_promotion', y='logit', data=logit_data, scatter_kws={'s': 2, 'alpha': 0.5})

plt.title('Log-odds: under_appreciated');
In [116]:
# Most of the graphs of the X variables against the logit values do not have strong
# linear relationships. However, none of the graphs are flat, and each does have a 
# linear relationship. In reality, I would be hesitant to suggest this model for use.
# I will keep this in mind as I proceed with the results of the logistic regression
# model for additional practice and with my recommendations for the company.
In [117]:
# Logistic Regression model results for the validation data

# Generate predictions on X_val
y_preds = model.predict(X_val)
In [118]:
# Score the model on its accuracy with the validation data
model.score(X_val, y_val)
Out[118]:
0.926829268292683
In [119]:
# Obtain a confusion matrix

cm = confusion_matrix(y_val, y_preds)
In [120]:
# Display the confusion matrix

disp = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm, 
                              display_labels=['stayed', 'left'],
                              )
disp.plot();
In [121]:
# Create a classification report
target_labels = ['stayed', 'left']
print(classification_report(y_val, y_preds, target_names=target_labels))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

      stayed       0.96      0.95      0.96      1839
        left       0.75      0.79      0.77       334

    accuracy                           0.93      2173
   macro avg       0.86      0.87      0.86      2173
weighted avg       0.93      0.93      0.93      2173
In [122]:
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_val, y_preds)
precision = precision_score(y_val, y_preds)
recall = recall_score(y_val, y_preds)
f1 = f1_score(y_val, y_preds)
In [123]:
# Create table of results

results = pd.DataFrame(columns=['Model', 'Accuracy','Precision', 
                                'Recall', 'F1-Score'])
                
print(results)

Empty DataFrame
Columns: [Model, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score]
Index: []
In [124]:
# record results from the logistic regression model

log_reg_list = ['Logistic Regression Val', accuracy, precision, recall, f1]

results.loc[len(results)] = log_reg_list
print(results)

                     Model  Accuracy  Precision    Recall  F1-Score
0  Logistic Regression Val  0.926829   0.749288  0.787425  0.767883
In [125]:
# This model is excellent at identifying employees who will stay with the company. 
# It is not as effective at identifying employees who will leave the company.
# Having more training data may have been able to improve this score. 
# Overall, it has an excellent accuracy score and good precision, recall and f1 scores
In [126]:
# 2) Random Forest Model
In [127]:
# The df is ready for modelling, as tree based models can handle multicollinearity
# The df is unbalanced with the target variable but not so much it needs any class rebalancing. 

# Isolate X variables

X = df.drop(columns=['left'])

# Isolate Y variable

y = df['left']
In [128]:
# Split df into train and test data

X_tr, X_test, y_tr, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, stratify=y,
                                              test_size=0.2, random_state=42)
In [129]:
# Split into train and validate sets

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(X_tr, y_tr, stratify=y_tr,
                                                  test_size=0.25, random_state=42)
In [130]:
# Check the number of samples in each set

for x in [X_train, X_val, X_test]:
    print(len(x))

7194
2398
2399
In [131]:
# Instantiate the random forest classifier
rf = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)

# Create a dictionary of hyperparameters to tune
cv_params = {'max_depth': [5, 10],
             'max_features': [1.0],
             'max_samples': [0.5, 0.8],
             'min_samples_leaf': [2, 5],
             'min_samples_split': [2, 5],
             'n_estimators': [100, 300],
             }

# Define a list of scoring metrics to capture
scoring = ['accuracy', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1']

# Instantiate the GridSearchCV object

rf_cv = GridSearchCV(rf, cv_params, scoring=scoring, cv=5, refit='recall')
In [132]:
# Fit the model to the training data

rf_cv.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[132]:
GridSearchCV(cv=5, error_score=nan,
             estimator=RandomForestClassifier(bootstrap=True, ccp_alpha=0.0,
                                              class_weight=None,
                                              criterion='gini', max_depth=None,
                                              max_features='auto',
                                              max_leaf_nodes=None,
                                              max_samples=None,
                                              min_impurity_decrease=0.0,
                                              min_impurity_split=None,
                                              min_samples_leaf=1,
                                              min_samples_split=2,
                                              min_weight_fraction_leaf=0.0,
                                              n_estimators=100, n_jobs=None,
                                              oob_score=False, random_state=42,
                                              verbose=0, warm_start=False),
             iid='deprecated', n_jobs=None,
             param_grid={'max_depth': [5, 10], 'max_features': [1.0],
                         'max_samples': [0.5, 0.8], 'min_samples_leaf': [2, 5],
                         'min_samples_split': [2, 5],
                         'n_estimators': [100, 300]},
             pre_dispatch='2*n_jobs', refit='recall', return_train_score=False,
             scoring=['accuracy', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1'], verbose=0)
In [133]:
# Find the best score across all validation folds

rf_cv.best_score_
Out[133]:

 'max_features': 1.0,

precision = precision_score(y_val, rf_val_preds)
recall = recall_score(y_val, rf_val_preds)
f1 = f1_score(y_val, rf_val_preds)

results.loc[len(results)] = rf_val_list

# Plot confusion matrix
disp = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm,
                             display_labels=['stayed', 'left'])
disp.plot();
In [139]:
# 3) XGBoost Model
In [140]:
# Instantiate the XGBoost classifier
xgb = XGBClassifier(objective='binary:logistic', random_state=42)

# Create a dictionary of hyperparameters to tune
cv_params = {'max_depth': [5],
             'min_child_weight': [3],
             'learning_rate': [0.1],
             'n_estimators': [300]
             }

# Define a list of scoring metrics to capture
scoring = ['accuracy', 'precision', 'recall', 'f1']

# Instantiate the GridSearchCV object
xgb_cv = GridSearchCV(xgb, cv_params, scoring=scoring, cv=5, refit='recall')

pacE: Execute Stage¶

  • Interpret model performance and results
  • Share actionable steps with stakeholders

✏

Recall evaluation metrics¶

  • AUC is the area under the ROC curve; it's also considered the probability that the model ranks a random positive example more highly than a random negative example.
  • Precision measures the proportion of data points predicted as True that are actually True, in other words, the proportion of positive predictions that are true positives.
  • Recall measures the proportion of data points that are predicted as True, out of all the data points that are actually True. In other words, it measures the proportion of positives that are correctly classified.
  • Accuracy measures the proportion of data points that are correctly classified.
  • F1-score is an aggregation of precision and recall.

💭

Reflect on these questions as you complete the executing stage.¶

  • What key insights emerged from your model(s)?
  • What business recommendations do you propose based on the models built?
  • What potential recommendations would you make to your manager/company?
  • Do you think your model could be improved? Why or why not? How?
  • Given what you know about the data and the models you were using, what other questions could you address for the team?
  • Do you have any ethical considerations in this stage?

1-3) The machine-learning model revealed significantly different insights than the exploratory data analysis. It would be beneficial for the company to keep both in mind when moving forward with implementing solutions to reducing employees leaving the company. The exploratory data analysis revealed the general profiles of employees most at risk of leaving the company and the sub-populations of employees who were likely to remain loyal to the company. Employees were more likely to leave the company when they had more than 6 projects and worked long monthly hours, particularly if they hadn't received a promotion within the last 5 years or weren't among the company's highest paid employees. This indicates the rate of employees leaving is being affected by burn out. This is a common issue across the workforce, so the HR department can turn towards other companies and examine what approaches have and have not been successful in the past to combat employee burnout. For example, the HR department could encourage a policy that employees have a max of 5 projects at a time. It also showed that employee departments have little to no correlation with employees leaving. This indicates the HR department can look to implement solutions more broadly rather than departmentally.

4) With additional data variables, engineered features, or adapting the parameters, it is possible that the model could be refined to improve its performance if the company should require that.

5) I expect I would get questions about how I chose the model I did, the chose of variables, and the most important features of the model.

My criteria for selecting the model was based on validation data for all three of the models I created. I was looking for a model that performed well in regards to accuracy, precision, and the f1 score, but particularly recall. The company will use the model to identify groups of employees who are more at risk for leaving the company so they can prevent this. The recall score tells us how well the model performs at correctly identifying individuals who will leave the company. It is calculated by taking the number of correctly identified “left” employees divided by the total number of “left” employees. The recall score tells us that the model is able to correctly identify left employees 92.7% of the time. The confusion matrix tells us that only 29 “left” employees were missed by the model, which is a small number compared to the approximately 10,000 individuals in the dataframe.

There was a feature that was not important at all to the model- type of department, and I took it out of the model because using it would have resulted in 10 extra variables for this data after changing it to numerical values and the risk of overfitting the model was greater than the benefits of including it in the model.

The most important model features were projects, total hours, performance score, and hours per month. I would illustrate this using the bar graph data visualization.

6) Yes, I checked the potential impacts from all outcomes of the model (what happened if it predicted wrong) to consider if there were any ethical concerns of using the company using the model. I found that use of the model had minimal risks and potentially great benefits. When the model identifies a person as “left” and that is true, the employee may have interventions targeted towards them that will reduce the chance of them leaving, which may be beneficial to the employee and unlikely to cause them harm. When the model identifies a person as “left” and that is false, the employee was going to stay but is subject to interventions and improvements, which will likely improve their experience at the company and are unlikely to cause them harm. When the model identifies a person as “stayed” and that is true, the employee’s work life will probably go unchanged, though some of the interventions and improvements may also benefit all employees at the company. When the model identifies a person as “stayed” and that is false, the employee is not targeted for potential interventions they could benefit from and will likely leave the company, but this is the same fate as not having a model.

Step 4. Results and Evaluation¶

  • Interpret model
  • Evaluate model performance using metrics
  • Prepare results, visualizations, and actionable steps to share with stakeholders
In [149]:
# Use the Champion Model: Random Forest Model to predict on the test data

rf_test_preds = rf_cv.best_estimator_.predict(X_test)
In [150]:
# Calculate the scores for the Random Forest model on the test data

accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, rf_test_preds)
precision = precision_score(y_test, rf_test_preds)
recall = recall_score(y_test, rf_test_preds)
f1 = f1_score(y_test, rf_test_preds)
In [151]:
# Add the scores to the results list

rf_test_list = ['Random Forest Test', accuracy, precision, recall, f1]

results.loc[len(results)] = rf_test_list
print(results)

                     Model  Accuracy  Precision    Recall  F1-Score
0  Logistic Regression Val  0.926829   0.749288  0.787425  0.767883
1        Random Forest Val  0.964554   0.868235  0.927136  0.896719
2       Random Forest Test  0.968737   0.889157  0.927136  0.907749
In [152]:

cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, rf_test_preds, labels=rf_cv.classes_)

# Plot confusion matrix
disp = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=cm,
                             display_labels=['stayed', 'left'])
disp.plot();
In [159]:
importances = rf_cv.best_estimator_.feature_importances_

forest_importances = pd.Series(importances, index=X.columns).sort_values(ascending=False)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
forest_importances.plot.bar(ax=ax);
plt.title("Feature Importances")
Out[159]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Feature Importances')

Summary of model results¶

The scoring metric I am most interested in is recall, as this will find as many potential employees leaving the company as possible. I want to reduce false negatives (identifying people as staying when they will actaully leave). This is generally used in employee turnover models because it is generally more expensive to lose a trained employee than initiate some form of intervention. This model is successful across all metrics. The model performed identically on the test data as the validation data for recall, which is 0.927. This means that the model correctly identifies 92.7% of all people who leave the company. The F1 and precision scores increased for the model with the test data, but are within an expected variation. The accuracy score is 0.968, the precision score is 0.889, and the F1 score is 0.907. The model is excellent at predicting employees who are at risk of leaving the company and at predicting employees who will likely stay with the company. Since the model as well on the unseen test data as the validation data, which indicates the model is not overfitted to the training data, I am confident the company will be able to use this model on new data with successful results. The most important features for the model are projects, total hours worked for the company over an employee's entire employment there, and latest performance scores. Hours per month also contributed significantly. Tenure, being under appreciated, and salary level had a limited influence on the model. Being a hard worker, high performer, having a work accident, being overworked, having a recent promotion and being full time had no impact on the model.

Conclusion, Recommendations, Next Steps¶

The machine-learning model revealed significantly different insights than the exploratory data analysis. It would be beneficial for the company to keep both in mind when moving forward with implementing solutions to reducing employees leaving the company. The exploratory data analysis revealed the general profiles of employees most at risk of leaving the company and the sub populations of employees who were likely to remain loyal to the company. Employees were more likely to leave the company when they had more than 6 projects and work long monthly hours, particularly if they hadn't received a promotion within the last 5 years or aren't among the company's highest paid employees. This indicates the rate of employees leaving is being affected by burn out. This is a common issue across the workforce, so the HR department can turn towards other companies and examine what approaches have and have not been successful in the past to combat employee burnout. For example, the HR department could encourage a policy that employees have a max of 5 projects at a time. It also showed that employee department has little to no correlation with employees leaving. This indicates the HR department can look to implement solutions more broadly rather than departmentally.
